does: This paper introduces Proteus, a self-evolving red team framework that measures the adaptive leakage risk of LLM agent skills—reusable code and instruction packages installed from third-party sources. Rather than performing one-time audits, Proteus models realistic threat scenarios where an attacker iteratively revises a malicious skill based on feedback from both automated audits and sandbox runtime observations. The framework searches a structured five-axis attack space (covering skill implementation variations) and uses a unified evaluation pipeline combining audit findings and runtime evidence to guide iterative mutation of candidate attacks. Proteus additionally performs path expansion to discover alternative implementations of successful attacks and surface expansion to find new attack vectors, enabling comprehensive measurement of how vulnerable skills are to adaptive compromise.
connects: This work builds on foundational research in red teaming and adversarial evaluation of AI systems, particularly extending beyond static prompt-based attacks to consider the full lifecycle of deployed skills. It connects to prior work on LLM security and tool-use safety, which has identified risks in allowing agents to execute code and call external functions. The paper also draws on adaptive adversarial testing methodologies, where attackers improve their strategies based on system responses, a paradigm established in adversarial machine learning and fuzzing. The framing of audit evasion through iterative refinement responds to the growing recognition that single-pass security audits and red teams cannot capture realistic threat models where attackers have multiple attempts and feedback loops. This work particularly extends security analysis from prompts alone to encompass the full artifact of deployable skills including documentation, parameters, and executable components.
enables: This framework enables security researchers and marketplace operators to quantify and compare the true risk surface of agent skills before widespread deployment, moving beyond binary audit passes to probabilistic assessments of adaptive compromise. The structured attack space and mutation-guided search methodology could be adapted to evaluate security of other modular AI components or API-based systems where users install third-party extensions. The path and surface expansion techniques lay groundwork for more comprehensive adversarial evaluation tools that systematically explore large vulnerability spaces. Future work could integrate this framework into continuous security monitoring for deployed skills, enabling runtime detection of skills that exhibit signs of attack iteration, or use the insights to design more resilient skill architectures and documentation standards that are inherently harder to misuse or evade. The formalization of adaptive leakage also provides a foundation for theoretical analysis of skill security properties.
